seed.title: FSD unlock consequence map
seed.prompt: Given the thesis that full self-driving crosses from maybe-someday to solved-enough deployment, surface consequences and select survivors under diverge and converge.
seed.thesis: Vision-based autonomy becoming legally and operationally real removes several substrates: crash risk, human driver time, traffic enforcement subjecthood, and private car ownership.
goals: prove one shared candidate pool can support both dials
keep novelty and grounding visible until selection
show regret siblings for what the other dial would have kept

candidates.title: Accident economy dries up
candidates.thesis: The visible crash reduction branch is not just safer roads; it removes revenue and labor from insurance, plaintiff law, collision repair, trauma workflows, and organ supply.
claims: Insurance and repair pools shrink when the crash event falls away.
Hidden dependents such as ad spend and trauma-side supply chains are downstream of accident volume.
evidence: large auto insurance and collision repair markets

candidates.title: Traffic-state loses its subject
candidates.thesis: When the human stops being the driver, the traffic stop, ticket, pretext-search, points, DUI docket, and forfeiture pipeline lose their ordinary on-ramp.
claims: A passenger cannot be cited as the driver by construction.
Fine revenue and pretext-stop authority face a dry-riverbed problem.
evidence: traffic stop as common police-citizen contact

candidates.title: Ownership becomes fleet yield
candidates.thesis: Autonomy attacks the privately owned idle car: finance, dealers, residual values, aftermarket repair, and parking all reprice around service fleets or owner-operated robotaxi yield.
claims: Idle time becomes monetizable only if the car can work without the owner.
The fork is pure service versus owned-autonomous fleet hybrid.
evidence: auto debt and franchise dealer structures

candidates.title: Sleeper commute rewires geography
candidates.thesis: Door-to-door autonomy changes the 200-700 mile band: sleeping or working through travel collapses the felt cost of distance and welds labor markets into megaregions.
claims: The strongest travel substitution is not urban robotaxi; it is intercity time conversion.
Real estate and labor pools reprice when commute radius stops meaning active driving.
evidence: door-to-door travel versus aviation weak bands

candidates.title: Adoption asymmetry creates border arbitrage
candidates.thesis: FSD arrives unevenly, so the edge lives at the boundary: believers versus disbelievers, served metros versus laggards, and leapfrog regions that skip old car ownership patterns.
claims: Average-arrival stories miss where the future is already real.
The boundary between served and unserved regions creates arbitrage.

candidates.title: Fleet compute becomes mobile edge
candidates.thesis: A large autonomous fleet could become a distributed sensing and compute substrate, turning parked or low-utilization vehicles into mobile edge infrastructure.
claims: Vehicles already carry sensors, power, and networked compute.
Idle fleet time might be a latent asset beyond transport.
evidence: speculative seam noted in FSD cluster

candidates.title: LiDAR retrofit inventory is not a lead
candidates.thesis: Competitors with deployed LiDAR-heavy fleets may own expensive inventory rather than durable advantage if camera-only OTA economics dominate the cost curve.
claims: Deployment count can mislead if the cost structure is wrong.
The curve matters more than static fleet snapshots.

candidates.title: Movement metering replaces fine revenue
candidates.thesis: The state will claw back dying fine, fuel-tax, and registration revenue through VMT, congestion pricing, and fleet-level movement metering.
claims: Governments do not let transport revenue lines die quietly.
Autonomous fleets make road-use pricing technically easier.
